title: Unit 11: Comparatives, superlatives, so, such, enough, too

grammar.usage: Used to compare two different people, things, or actions.
grammar.example: He is taller than his brother.

grammar.usage: Used to compare one person, thing, or action in a group with all others in that group.
grammar.example: She is the most intelligent student in our class.

grammar.usage: Used to express the result or consequence of an action or situation.
grammar.example: The movie was so interesting that I watched it twice.

grammar.usage: Enough is used to show sufficiency. Too is used to express excess, often with a negative meaning.
grammar.example: He is not old enough to drive a car / The tea is too hot to drink.

rows:
"than" always follows the comparative
EX: She is older than me.

Always has "the" in front of the superlative
EX: She is the oldest.

good → better
bad → worse
far → farther / further
little → less
much / many → more

good → best
bad → worst
far → farthest / furthest
little → least
much / many → most

More / less + adv
EX: She runs more quickly than me.

Most / least + adv
EX: He speaks most eloquently of all.

badly → worse
early → earlier
far → farther / further
fast → faster
hard → harder
late → later
often → more often
near → nearer
soon → sooner
well → better

badly → worst
early → earliest
far → farthest / furthest
fast → fastest
hard → hardest
late → latest
often → most often
near → nearest
soon → soonest
well → best
Expresses result/consequence of an action or situation
Expresses result/consequence of an action or situation

so + adj / adv (that)
so + adv + that
so + many / much + N + that

such + a/an + adj + N (singular countable) + that
such + adj + N (plural/uncountable) + that
such + a lot of + N + that
Expresses the result when there is a sufficient quantity of something
Expresses that something has more than the normal/reasonable amount

enough + N (+ for and/or + Vbare)
adj + enough (+ for and/or + Vbare)
adv + enough (+ for and/or + Vbare)

too + adj (+ for and/or + Vbare)
too + adv (+ for and/or + Vbare)
too + many / much + N (+ for and/or + Vbare)

bullets: In the structure 'so / such + adj / adv (+ that)', 'that' can be omitted.

If the main verb in the sentence is a linking verb (like 'to be', 'look', 'seem', etc.), we use an adjective (adj).
EX: The weather is so nice today.

If the main verb is an action verb, we use an adverb (adv).
EX: He drives so quickly that I get scared.

rows: It's enough dangerous around here so don't go out alone.

It's dangerous enough around here so don't go out alone.
OR: It's quite / fairly dangerous around here so don't go out alone.
Negative nuance of 'too' vs positive adjectives
You were too lucky not to get caught.
You were very / really / extremely lucky not to get caught. ('too' usually implies a negative consequence, so 'too lucky' is unnatural here)

vocabulary.meaning: to go or come before something else in time, order, or position
vocabulary.example: The performance will be preceded by a short introductory speech.

vocabulary.meaning: to enter a building illegally, usually using force, and steal things
vocabulary.example: The house was burgled while the family was away on holiday.

vocabulary.meaning: a group of criminals who break into buildings to steal things
vocabulary.example: The police caught a gang of burglars operating in the neighborhood.

vocabulary.meaning: the main story of a book, film, or play, or a secret plan to do something illegal
vocabulary.example: The movie has a very complex plot with many unexpected twists.

vocabulary.meaning: to become available to the public (like a book or movie), or to appear
vocabulary.example: When does the new album of the band come out?

vocabulary.meaning: a four-wheeled carriage for a baby, pushed by a person on foot
vocabulary.example: She pushed the sleeping baby in the pram through the park.

vocabulary.meaning: to take hold of something or someone suddenly and roughly
vocabulary.example: He grabbed his coat and keys and ran out of the door.
